topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany gleamed under the soft lighting of Dr. Thorne’s office, a space that felt far too polished for the rough edges of my memory. I stood awkwardly by the door, my hands shoved deep into my pockets, a faint tremor running through my fingers. Across the room, sitting behind a desk that looked more like a throne, sat Mark. Mark, the architect of my early misery, now possessed an air of quiet, almost unsettling calm. He wore a tailored suit, and his expression, when he finally looked up, held a detached, professional empathy that made my jaw tighten. "Come in," he said, his voice low and even, devoid of any familiar mockery. I shuffled forward, the expensive carpet muffling my footsteps. I kept my gaze fixed on the pattern in the rug, unwilling to meet those eyes that used to ignite a familiar, hot resentment in my chest.

[story 2]

I watched him across the therapy couch, a knot tightening in my stomach with every deliberate, measured sentence he spoke. Mark, who used to chase me down the halls, now spoke about trauma and coping mechanisms with the precision of a seasoned professional. Every anecdote he shared about his own difficult past felt like a calculated performance, designed to elicit a sympathetic response from me. I shifted uncomfortably, pulling my knees up to my chest, a defensive posture. "So, you think," I managed, my voice sounding brittle, "that all of that is just... manageable?" I waited for the inevitable smirk, the familiar challenge, but only received a gentle, almost pitying nod. The air in the room felt thick, heavy with unspoken expectations, and I found myself wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

It was impossible to reconcile the boy who had once belittled me with the man who now held a degree and a title. I observed Mark meticulously taking notes, his pen scratching softly against the paper. My shoulders tensed; I crossed my arms tightly over my chest, a physical barrier against the intrusion of his perceived understanding. When he finally addressed me, his tone was measured, almost clinical, completely lacking the sharp edge I remembered. "Tell me," he prompted, leaning forward slightly, "what specific interactions from your childhood feel unresolved?" The question felt like an accusation, a demand for vulnerability I was utterly unwilling to grant. I stared at the polished wood of the coffee table, my breathing shallow, feeling a profound sense of being utterly exposed by his calm scrutiny.
